input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The study focuses on a building infrastructure within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain. The physical twin represents a complex building whose cyber‑physical assets include sensors, actuators, and controllers modeled directly within an enriched BIM environment. The system is a multi‑characteristic building that requires continuous real‑time data exchange, predictive analytics, and control for efficient operation across its life‑cycle, making it a suitable candidate for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Monitoring and diagnostics of building states through continuous data ingestion; real‑time visualization of structural and operational metrics via immersive interfaces; predictive analytics that generate alerts and optimization suggestions for HVAC, lighting, and energy usage; control services that allow Facility Managers to trigger actuators and adjust controller parameters directly from the twin; and extensible analytics plugins that DT Engineers can develop to add domain‑specific analytics. Target users are Building Control Engineers (device and control logic configuration), Facility Managers (system state monitoring and operation), and DT Engineers (custom service development).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The architecture employs Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to translate enriched BIM models into deployable Digital Twin artifacts.  Runtime data is synchronised through a lightweight, real‑time exchange layer, while an interactive immersive interface delivers visualization to users.  The modular design exposes extension points enabling DT Engineers to integrate additional analytic or service modules.  No proprietary software or specific stack components are explicitly named in the documents considered.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real‑time data exchange enables continuous synchronization between the physical building and its digital counterpart; the system operates in a real‑time, continuous mode without explicitly defined polling intervals, aligning with the real‑time monitoring and control requirements outlined in the framework.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system is orchestrated through a modular architecture featuring a central DT Hub that manages component integration via standardized extension points. Runtime configuration management allows dynamic adjustments of services and analytics without redeployment, while resource management is coordinated centrally to support scalability from individual components up to complete infrastructure systems. The hub mediates all data exchange, maintaining system-wide coordination and ensuring consistent lifecycle management from enriched BIM inputs to operational control.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The framework achieves interoperability across heterogeneous systems through standardized extension points and support for custom services and analytics. Bidirectional data exchange facilitates integration with external platforms and enterprise systems, and the modular design allows seamless incorporation of additional Digital Twins or third‑party services, ensuring cohesive horizontal integration within the broader AECO ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin framework derives its computational models from enriched Building Information Models (BIM) that embed device, sensor, actuator, and controller metadata directly within the BIM environment. During deployment, a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline validates the enriched BIM, converts it into a structured representation, and automatically generates deployable Digital Twin artifacts. The resulting models include geometric representations (from the BIM geometry), behavioural models (via TwinComponent representations that link 3D visual objects to physical devices), and analytical models accessible through custom services built with the framework’s extension points. Runtime data are managed through Eclipse Ditto, which stores the state of each device instance and enforces policy‑based validation of sensor values (including ranges and units). Real‑time telemetry is routed between the physical building and its twin through Eclipse Hono’s MQTT broker, which delivers events with an at‑least‑once guarantee and writes them to Ditto’s persistent event store for audit and recovery.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is specified in the BIM model by Building Control Engineers and materialised in the Digital Twin through dedicated TwinComponent modules. For lighting, a LightTwinComponent maps a UE5 light to its mirrored physical light fixture, exposing control parameters such as on/off, brightness, and colour‑temperature (in Kelvin). Commands originate from the UnrealTwinController, which forwards messages via DittoController to the corresponding DittoDevice representing the physical actuator. Similar mechanisms are provisioned for HVAC and other devices via the framework’s HVACFeatureParser and HVACTwinComponent placeholders. Safety constraints are enforced by Ditto’s policy‑based validation during the pre‑execution phase, rejecting command values that fall outside declared operational ranges and preventing unsafe actuation sequences. Actuation ranges for each device are defined in the BIM metadata and enforced during runtime.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined and configured in BIM using metadata that specifies the measurement type, unit, and physical location. When the framework processes the enriched BIM, each sensor is instantiated as a DittoDevice with an associated DittoConnection. Sensor telemetry is published in real‑time through Hono’s MQTT broker to the Digital Twin, where it is validated against policy rules (e.g., acceptable value bounds and units). The framework supports bidirectional communication, allowing the twin to request configuration updates from the physical device and to observe state changes instantly. Although the documents do not provide explicit sampling rates or transmission bandwidths, the use of Hono ensures reliable, at‑least‑once delivery of measurement events, and the integration with UE5 permits interactive visualization of sensor data in the immersive rendering scenes.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through a combination of enriched BIM semantics, MDE‑generated twin artifacts, and runtime validation. Eclipse Ditto’s policy‑based validation enforces numerical ranges and unit consistency for all sensor readings before they reach the application layer. Pre‑execution checks on actuator commands prevent violations of safe operating limits. Fault tolerance is achieved via Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery, guaranteeing that measurement or command events are not lost even in transient network failures; Ditto’s persistent event storage captures all events for eventual consistency and replay. The modular architecture allows developers to inject additional validation layers (e.g., FeatureParser to interpret domain‑specific features) and analytics services, thereby supporting continuous quality assurance throughout the twin’s life cycle.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework follows a multi‑stage lifecycle starting from enrichment of the Building Information Model (BIM) with detailed device, sensor, actuator and controller definitions performed by the Building Control Engineer. In the Design + Generation stage, a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) engine parses the enriched BIM and generates Structured Representation Models (SRMs) that map directly to runtime digital objects. The Deploy stage automatically materialises these SRMs into deployable DT artifacts (containers, services and publish/subscribe interfaces) with minimal human intervention. Continuous Operations are maintained through real‑time data exchange layers that keep the physical hardware states in sync with their digital twins, while an interactive visualization portal provides immersive monitoring and control for Facility Managers. Governance is split by stakeholder role: the Building Control Engineer validates model integrity, the Facility Manager governs day‑to‑day operation, and the DT Engineer oversees extension points and versioning of custom plugins. Transition management is achieved through the MDE transform pipeline and a modular extension registry that facilitates rollback or progressive upgrade of deployed artifacts.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The framework adopts a model‑driven engineering methodology: (1) device modeling capabilities are embedded directly into BIM, enabling control logic definition; (2) an MDE pipeline automatically extracts and transforms the enriched BIM into DT artifacts, reducing manual interventions; (3) the transformation process includes structural validation of the model-to-physical mapping, serving as a basic quality assurance step; (4) deployable DT artifacts are generated with minimal configuration, and the modular architecture provides extension points for custom services and analytics; (5) the process supports evolution through runtime extension, but specific version‑control mechanisms are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real‑time monitoring, simulation and interactive visualization features support decision making. The DT exposes live sensor data and allows operators to perform in‑twin simulations of control logic, enabling predictive troubleshooting. Custom analytics plugins can be loaded via the DT Engineer extension points to generate KPI dashboards and alerts, and recommendations are presented through immersive visualization interfaces. Confidence metrics or formal decision‑making algorithms are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework is grounded in the AECO‑specific BIM standard, using BIM models as the primary semantic foundation for device and control definitions. It leverages Model‑Driven Engineering principles that integrate with standard data modeling practices. Additionally, the framework references open‑source DT connectivity solutions such as Eclipse Ditto and BaSyx, both of which have been noted in the literature for providing robust connectivity and compliance with industry‑wide standards, thereby addressing the previously identified lack of standardized data schemas and bidirectional communication protocols. The use of these interoperable components and the explicit alignment with BIM signify adherence to recognized AECO and general IoT interoperability standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the domain (AECO) and the nature of the physical twin (a building infrastructure requiring real-time data exchange). It correctly identifies the inclusion of sensors, actuators, and controllers modeled within BIM. However, it misses the specific name of the case study ('Living Lab') mentioned in the text, and 'multi-characteristic building' is a slight paraphrase of the text's description of complex infrastructure.

The extraction correctly identifies the services (monitoring, visualization, control, extensibility) and accurately maps the target users (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) to their roles based on Table I and the text. However, it adds specific details about 'predictive analytics generating alerts for HVAC' which, while mentioned generally in the introduction and related work, is not explicitly detailed as a specific service output of the proposed framework in the same way the core services are.

The description claims that 'No proprietary software or specific stack components are explicitly named,' which directly contradicts the source text. The text explicitly names Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, and Unreal Engine as key enablers. The description correctly mentions MDE and BIM but fails to identify the specific tools outlined in the document. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'Unreal Engine... provides a rich visualization environment'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The description provides a highly specific and detailed summary of the system's orchestration. It accurately reflects the 'modular architecture with defined extension points' (R4), 'Runtime configuration management' enabling 'adjustments without requiring full redeployment' (Table III), and the central coordination model where 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub' (Section III.B).

The extraction accurately captures the integration approach described in the text. It aligns with the 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' requirement (Section III.A) and the use of 'standardized extension points' to support 'modular, service-oriented DT platforms' (Abstract, R4). It also correctly identifies the role of 'bidirectional data exchange' (Section II) for integration.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents discuss a 'model-driven deployment framework' and 'automated deployment process', but they do not specify hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational/storage requirements, or specific deployment models. The text focuses on the software methodology rather than the hosting infrastructure. Therefore, the claim that this information is not in the document is correct.

Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
